title: Italic minuscule, the chancery hand
subtitle: A compressed, forward-sloping alphabet from the Italian Renaissance
excerpt: Italic is the hand of the Italian Renaissance chanceries: slightly compressed, forward-sloping at 5 to 10° from vertical, written at a 45° nib angle. It is faster than Foundational, more rhythmic, and historically the bridge between formal calligraphy and the italic typefaces still in common use. The twenty-six lower-case letters derive from a compressed oval rather than the Foundational circle.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), chapter on italic and the humanistic cursive tradition. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/writingilluminat00john. Historical model: the chancery scripts of Rome in the fifteenth and sixteenth centuries, documented in facsimile publications of Papal Chancery documents that are in the public domain by age.

glossaryTerms.definition: The forward angle of Italic letterforms from the vertical, typically 5 to 10°. This slope is a structural feature of the hand, not a by-product of fast writing.

In Italic, the arch that connects the stem to the second leg of letters like n, m, h. Unlike the Foundational arch which lifts from inside the stem, the Italic arch branches from just above the baseline before climbing.
The angle of the broad nib's edge to the baseline. Italic uses ~45° throughout, higher than Foundational's 30°.
The height of the main body of lower-case letters, in nib widths. Italic lower-case is typically 5 nib widths, one more than Foundational.

content.text: Italic is the Foundational hand compressed laterally by approximately 20% and leaned forward at a consistent

rises to 45°, which changes the distribution of thick and thin strokes: the verticals become slightly thinner and the diagonals slightly thicker than in Foundational. The result is a lighter, more rhythmic page texture.

items.name: Mitchell Roundhand nib, size 3 (or Brause Bandzug for crisper edge)
Cartridge paper, 130 gsm, guide lines ruled at 5 nib widths x-height

content.text: Before the first stroke, draw a series of slope guidelines in pencil: lines at 5° to 10° from the vertical, crossing the horizontal guide lines at regular intervals. These are not the letter stems, they are reference lines to check that each downstroke leans at the right angle. The slope is consistent throughout the alphabet: every vertical stem in every letter leans to the right at the same angle.

The Italic o is a compressed oval, not a circle. It is roughly two-thirds as wide as it is tall, and it leans forward at the italic slope. Write it in two strokes: the left arc descends from the 1-o'clock position to the 7-o'clock position; the right arc descends from the 1-o'clock position to the 5-o'clock position. The thickest parts are at the left and right sides of the oval, but because the nib angle is 45° rather than 30°, the thick-to-thin transition is more gradual.

in letters like n, h, m springs from very low on the stem, approximately 25% of the x-height from the baseline, not from the top half of the letter as in Foundational. The arch curves outward and upward before descending straight to the baseline. This branching angle gives Italic its characteristic flowing, rhythmic quality.

at 5 nib widths for the standard Italic proportions. Ascenders rise to 8 nib widths; descenders fall to 3 nib widths below the baseline. The extra nib width of x-height compared to Foundational makes the letters slightly lighter in weight, the letter body is taller relative to the nib width, so there is more internal white space.

items.symptom: The letters appear to lean at inconsistent angles, some vertical, some too far forward
items.cause: The slope is not held consistently. The hand is allowing the pen to drift upright between letters.
items.fix: Rule slope guide lines across the practice sheet and check every downstroke against them. Practise the downstroke alone as a warmup: fill a line with evenly spaced parallel downstrokes at the correct slope before adding any letter bodies.

items.symptom: The Italic looks identical to Foundational, the compression is not showing
items.cause: The o oval has not been compressed, it is being written as a circle.
items.fix: Consciously narrow the rightward travel of the right-arc stroke. The width of the Italic o should be approximately 60 to 65% of its height. Compare against the model: if the o reads as round, it is not compressed enough.
